topic.theme: Theme 1 · Introduction to Markets and Market Failure
topic.title: Demand
topic.goal: Lock in the demand curve: why it slopes down, movements along it vs shifts of it, the conditions of demand, and the types of good – the foundations of all market analysis.

intro.summary: Demand is the quantity of a good consumers are willing and able to buy at each possible price. The demand curve is the most-used diagram in economics – the non-negotiable skill is telling what moves ALONG it (a price change) from what SHIFTS it (a non-price determinant).
intro.doInThis: Work through 8 cards: the big picture, why the curve slopes down, movements along the curve, shifts of the curve, what shifts demand, types of good, derived demand, and an exam toolkit.
outcomes: Distinguish a movement along the demand curve from a shift of the demand curve
Explain why the demand curve slopes down (substitution, real-income, diminishing marginal utility)
List and apply the conditions of demand that shift the curve
Classify normal, inferior, substitute and complement goods and predict the shift
intro.tip: A change in the good’s own price causes a movement ALONG the demand curve. A change in any other determinant (income, tastes, related-goods prices) shifts the WHOLE curve.

cards.title: Demand: the big picture

tip.text: Demand means willingness AND ability to pay – not just wanting something. The demand curve shows how much consumers buy at each price.

views.label: The demand curve
views.head: The demand curve
views.body: Demand shows how much consumers are willing and able to buy at each price – the whole downward-sloping curve D.

views.label: A higher price → less
views.head: At a higher price (A)
views.body: At the higher price of £10 (point A), quantity demanded is just 20.

views.label: A lower price → more
views.head: At a lower price (B)
views.body: At the lower price of £6 (point B), quantity demanded rises to 40 – the law of demand.

causes.head: Ability
causes.body: They can afford to buy it.

causes.head: At each price
causes.body: Demand is a relationship across prices, not a single number.

keyTerms.def: The whole relationship between price and quantity demanded.
The amount consumers buy at one particular price.
All other influences on demand are held constant.

cards.examEdge: Use quantity demanded for movements along the curve; reserve demand for the whole curve or a shift. A price change moves us along the curve; a non-price factor shifts the whole curve.

cards.title: Why the demand curve slopes down

tip.text: As price falls consumers usually buy more; as price rises they usually buy less. Lower prices make a good both more attractive and more affordable.

interactiveDiagram.label: Why quantity demanded rises as price falls

views.label: Substitution effect
views.head: ① Substitution effect
views.body: At a high price (A, £10 → 20), the good is dear relative to substitutes. As it gets cheaper, consumers switch towards it, so quantity demanded rises.

views.label: Real-income effect
views.head: ② Real-income effect
views.body: A lower price (B, £8 → 30) means each £ stretches further – real purchasing power rises, so consumers can afford more.

views.label: Diminishing marginal utility
views.head: ③ Diminishing marginal utility
views.body: Each extra unit gives less satisfaction, so consumers only buy more (C, £6 → 40) if the price falls – which is exactly the downward slope.

causes.head: Substitution effect
causes.body: When the good gets cheaper, consumers switch towards it and away from alternatives.

causes.head: Real-income effect
causes.body: A lower price stretches purchasing power, so consumers can afford more.

causes.head: Diminishing marginal utility
causes.body: Extra units are valued less, so consumers only buy more if the price falls.

causes2.head: Lower price
causes2.body: A movement down the curve – quantity demanded extends.

causes2.head: Higher price
causes2.body: A movement up the curve – quantity demanded contracts.

causes2.head: Usually, not always
causes2.body: The law of demand holds in most cases; Giffen and Veblen goods are rare exceptions.

tipLate.head: The deepest reason
tipLate.text: Diminishing marginal utility is the foundational reason demand curves slope down. Each extra unit gives less satisfaction, so consumers only choose to buy more if the price falls. Substitution and real-income effects sit on top of this – DMU is the bedrock that makes the law of demand hold even when no clear substitute or income change is involved.

cards.examEdge: When explaining the slope, name at least one mechanism – the substitution effect, the real-income effect, or diminishing marginal utility. The spec (1.2.2) specifically requires you to link DMU to the demand-curve shape, so this is the safest move. Rare Giffen/Veblen exceptions don’t overturn the basic demand story.
